original_abstract:
Standardized food reference amounts—such as 100 grams, 100 kilocalories, or the FDA’s Reference Amount Customarily Consumed (RACC)—often misrepresent the actual nutrient density of foods. Relying on these metrics for dietary choices can inadvertently increase the risk of chronic disease by promoting foods that are either excessive in energy or deficient in key nutrients. This study evaluated the nutritional quality and component levels of 8,596 food items across 25 categories using guidelines from the Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC), the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA), and a newly proposed assessment procedure. 

Our findings reveal significant vulnerabilities in current regulatory frameworks. Under both FDA and CAC standards, selecting foods to meet targets for "positive" components (such as protein, fiber, vitamins, and minerals) frequently resulted in energy intakes that far exceeded daily requirements. Conversely, the CAC criteria often failed to identify foods that truly satisfy these positive nutrient demands. We also observed "negative" component overages: many foods categorized as suitable under CAC or FDA guidelines actually surpassed recommended limits for cholesterol, sodium, and saturated fat. 

Furthermore, current reference amounts led to paradoxical quality rankings. Due to the limitations of weight- and calorie-based scaling, fast foods (when analyzed by serving size), spices (when analyzed per 100g), and vegetables (when analyzed per 100 kcal) received disproportionately high nutritional quality scores. By contrast, our suggested procedure provides a more reliable framework for children and adults by accounting for energy density and realistic consumption patterns, ensuring that nutrient targets are met without promoting caloric excess or chronic disease risk.
humanized_abstract:
Common standardized food reference amounts, such as 100g or 100kcal, or the FDA’s Reference Amount Customarily Consumed (RACC) are frequently not representative of the actual nutrient content of food products. Using these reference amounts for dietary decision-making may therefore pose a risk for increasing chronic diseases, particularly through the overconsumption of energy and the resulting underconsumption of essential nutrients. This study evaluated the nutritional value and content of nutrients for 8,596 food items across 25 categories according to the Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC) guidelines, FDA guidelines, and in accordance with a new proposed method.

Our study found major shortcomings in current regulatory approaches. When the targets for the 'positive' nutrients such as protein, fiber, vitamins and minerals were met, in all cases the calculated energy intake was more than threefold higher than the recommended intake levels, both under FDA and CAC regulatory frameworks. Also, the CAC approach failed to adequately cover the positive targets of nutrients. Excessive levels of the 'negative' nutrients, such as cholesterol, sodium and saturated fats were also observed, even when foods complied with the CAC and FDA approaches.

The current reference amounts also led to a number of paradoxical quality rankings for foods, which are inherent to the weight- or calorie-based scaling procedures used. For example, faster foods, when evaluated per serving, spices when evaluated per 100 g, and vegetables when evaluated per 100 kcal gave extremely high nutritional quality scores. The suggested new procedure will help to eliminate these anomalies and provide a more sound basis for nutrition evaluation for children and adults that takes into account energy density of foods and more appropriate servings based on real eating patterns to assure that recommended nutrient levels are provided without promoting excessive energy intake or known risk of chronic diseases.
